af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Firebase Cloud Messaging for cross-platform push notifications. Service account (Admin SDK) recommended for agents — avoids user interaction. Legacy HTTP API deprecated; HTTP v1 API preferred. 1000 messages/sec per project limit. Topic-based and token-based delivery. Notification vs Data messages behave differently on app foreground/background. Registration token management required — tokens expire and rotate. Multi-language Firebase Admin SDK available.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Service account + OAuth2 for Admin SDK access. Google-maintained infrastructure. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR compliance. Service account JSON via secrets manager — never commit to source. FCM scope limited to messaging — no cross-service access. Legacy server key deprecated (avoid). Token-level delivery enables per-device targeting. Excellent security posture as Google Cloud service.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Cloud infrastructure with SLA. v1 API stable; legacy deprecated with migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Delivery not guaranteed (push is best-effort). INVALID_ARGUMENT errors for bad tokens require token cleanup. Batch messaging available for efficiency. 1000 msg/sec limit requires rate management for high-volume agents. Token rotation means stored tokens may expire.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) is a standard, not a single API — each health system implements it separately. SMART on FHIR OAuth2 is the most complex auth pattern for agents. OperationOutcome provides structured errors. Rate limits vary per implementation. FHIR R4 is current stable version (STU3 still widely deployed). System-level JWT auth available for backend agents (no user interaction). HIPAA compliance mandatory for PHI. Resource types (Patient, Observation, etc.) follow a standard structure.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS required by FHIR spec for PHI. SMART on FHIR OAuth2 with fine-grained clinical scopes (patient/Patient.read, system/Observation.read, etc.). HIPAA BAA required for any production PHI access. Implementation-dependent security — each FHIR server varies. OAuth2 tokens via secure flow. Scope granularity enables per-resource-type access control. System JWT auth uses RSA key pairs requiring HSM or secrets manager.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Depends entirely on FHIR server implementation — no single SLA. FHIR standard provides cross-implementation stability. R4 versioning stable; R4/STU3 both maintained with migration guides. Retry guidance in FHIR spec. OperationOutcome errors are structured and retryable. Rate limits per implementation require pre-negotiation. Search operations idempotent. FHIR server availability varies across health system deployments.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with moderate quality (58). Aggregates financial data from multiple sources (stock prices, news, fundamentals). API key required. Rate limits vary by underlying data provider — often undocumented. Financial data accuracy and freshness varies by data source. Market hours affect data availability. Real-time vs delayed data distinction important for agent decisions. Limited documentation beyond basic tool listing. Useful for financial analysis workflows but verify data source reliability before production use.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS to financial data providers. API key auth — no scope granularity. Unknown version and dependency audit status. No formal compliance certifications listed. API key via environment variable. Financial data aggregation means trusting multiple upstream data sources. Data accuracy for trading decisions carries legal and financial risk — verify data quality before production use.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS dependent on multiple financial data providers. Unknown version — may have breaking changes. No documented change policy. Market data queries idempotent. After-hours data availability limited. Data provider outages propagate to MCP server. Rate limits from underlying providers cause silent failures. Market data freshness varies — real-time vs 15-min delayed must be tracked per source.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Comprehensive financial data API — real-time stock prices, fundamentals, earnings, economic data, news sentiment. Simple API key. Free tier: 60 calls/min with 30 calls/sec burst. WebSocket available for real-time quotes — different protocol from REST. Symbol lookup required for less common tickers. International markets available with coverage gaps. Webhook support for earnings and market events. Excellent breadth for financial data use cases.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS and WSS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. Finnhub maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed. API key via environment variable. Read-only financial data — no write operations reduce risk. Financial data used for trading decisions may have regulatory implications in some jurisdictions. API key in query parameter (also acceptable in header).

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Good backward compatibility. Retry guidance documented. Full GET idempotency on all market data queries. Free tier rate limits (60/min) constraining for polling agents — use WebSocket for real-time. After-hours and weekend availability limited for market data. International coverage gaps may cause silent failures for non-US symbols.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Firebase Authentication — user management, custom tokens, ID token verification. Service account (Admin SDK) is the right pattern for agents managing users. API key for client-side auth (not appropriate for server-side agent use). Admin SDK enables user creation, deletion, custom claims, token verification. Rate limits per project — 1 request/sec for some operations. Multi-factor authentication management available. Token verification can be done locally (no API call needed).

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Service account for admin operations; ID tokens for user context. Admin/client separation prevents privilege escalation. Google-maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR/HIPAA (with BAA) compliance. Service account JSON via secrets manager. Custom claims enable role-based access control. ID token verification can be done without API calls using Firebase's public keys — reduces latency and rate limit exposure.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Cloud infrastructure with SLA. Admin SDK stable across versions. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Token verification idempotent. User creation not idempotent — check existence before creating. Rate limits on some admin operations (1/sec) require serialization for batch operations. Service account token auto-refresh in Admin SDK simplifies reliability.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Firestore is Google's scalable NoSQL document database. Three auth paths: service account (agents), Firebase Auth token (user-context), API key (client-side only). Service account + Admin SDK is correct for server-side agents. Security Rules enforce access control. Transactions for atomic operations. Subcollections allow hierarchical data. Real-time listeners via gRPC streams. 1 write/second per document limit is a common surprise for agents. Composite indexes must be pre-created.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Service account with Firestore Security Rules for document-level access control. Fine-grained per-collection and per-field permissions via Security Rules. Google-maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR/HIPAA (with BAA) compliance. Service account JSON via secrets manager. Admin SDK bypasses Security Rules — must implement application-level access control for admin agents. Excellent security architecture for a managed database.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Cloud infrastructure with SLA. Stable Admin SDK versioning. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. ACID transactions for atomic operations. 1 write/sec per document limit requires design consideration for high-frequency agents. Idempotent reads; non-idempotent writes (use transactions for safety). Composite indexes must be defined before queries that require them — index errors are common gotcha.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Firebase Realtime Database — JSON tree structure, WebSocket-based real-time sync. Older than Firestore; fewer features but simpler data model. Database Secret is DEPRECATED — use service account or Firebase Auth tokens. REST API available for agents that don't need real-time. Security Rules for path-based access control. 100 concurrent connections on free tier. Shallow reads for large datasets. Firestore preferred for new projects unless real-time sync is essential.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS and WSS enforced. Firebase Auth tokens or service account for auth. Security Rules provide path-level access control. Google-maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR compliance. Database Secret is deprecated legacy auth — avoid entirely. Service account JSON via secrets manager. Security Rules inheritance in JSON tree can cause unintended access if not carefully designed. Admin SDK bypasses Security Rules.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Cloud infrastructure with SLA. Stable REST API. Good migration guides (Realtime DB to Firestore). Retry guidance documented. Connection limits (100 on free tier) require management for concurrent agents. Data operations idempotent on REST. Real-time listeners require reconnection handling. WebSocket reconnection is automatic in SDK but custom REST polling needs retry logic. Unlimited document writes (unlike Firestore's 1/sec per doc limit).

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with good quality (80). Purpose-built web scraping and crawling API for LLM workflows — returns clean Markdown by default. Simple API key. Crawl jobs are async — agents must poll or use webhooks. Screenshot capture available. Custom extraction with LLM-powered structured data parsing. Rate limits by plan tier. JavaScript rendering included by default. Sitemap auto-discovery for full site crawls. Self-hostable for production workloads. Excellent for web data extraction in agent pipelines.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. Firecrawl maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed. API key via environment variable. Crawling external URLs on behalf of agent raises ToS considerations for target sites. Scraped data may contain PII — handle according to applicable data protection laws. Self-hosted option available for data residency requirements.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable versioned API. Good backward compatibility. Retry guidance documented. Async crawl jobs require polling — implement with timeouts. GET status queries idempotent. Rate limits by plan can cause 429 errors requiring backoff. JavaScript-heavy sites add processing time and failure modes. Some sites block scrapers despite Firecrawl's headers — handle access denial gracefully.

af_score_components.notes: Official Firecrawl MCP server (quality 80). Same API key as base Firecrawl API. Exposes scrape, crawl, search, and extract tools. Async crawl jobs require agents to handle job IDs and poll for completion within MCP tool calls. Tool descriptions auto-generated from Firecrawl API spec. MCP server adds an abstraction layer over the REST API — minor overhead. Self-hosted version of server also available via npx. Very similar to firecrawl-api (same underlying service, MCP wrapper).

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS to Firecrawl API. API key via FIRECRAWL_API_KEY env var. Same security posture as base Firecrawl API — MCP wrapper doesn't change security boundaries. No additional auth layer added by MCP server. Tool call parameter validation relies on Firecrawl API validation. Crawled URL data handled per Firecrawl data policies.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Reliability tied to Firecrawl API. MCP server versioning may lag behind API updates. Async crawl operations require careful state management within agent workflows. Error propagation from API through MCP adds debugging complexity. Rate limits from base API apply. MCP server itself is a thin wrapper — low failure surface beyond network calls.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Data integration platform for syncing data to warehouses. API key + secret (Base64 encoded together as Basic Auth). Connector management, sync triggering, and status monitoring are primary agent use cases. Sync operations are async — agents must poll sync status. Schema changes in source systems can break connectors silently. Webhook support for sync completion events. Rate limits documented. Free tier limited to 10 connectors.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key + secret as Basic Auth — both credentials required. Limited scope granularity. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR compliance for data pipeline platform. API key and secret via environment variables. Data pipeline access means agents can trigger syncs to destination warehouses — significant data movement capability. Read-only API key option available for monitoring workflows.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Enterprise SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioned API. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Async sync operations require polling — implement with appropriate timeouts. Sync status queries idempotent. Schema drift in source systems causes connector failures requiring manual intervention. Historical sync re-runs are expensive and must be used carefully. Connector-level error messages often require debugging at source system.
